explanation: This strong verb conveys a deep sense of moral and personal violation regarding the betrayal of his true creative nature.
Orwell uses this to describe his childhood isolation, setting the emotional foundation for his retreat into a private world of words.
This word highlights a specific social pain that fueled his early literary ambitions as a way to seek validation.
It signals his commitment to truth over comfort, a core emotional and intellectual driver throughout his career.
Used to describe his failed early short stories, this hyperbolic term reflects his harsh self-criticism and high standards.
This indicates a sense of disdain for his early, less serious work, showing a shift toward more meaningful content.
This suggests an external, irresistible force driving his writing, moving beyond simple desire to a psychological necessity.
Used to describe his reaction to Milton's poetry, this word captures the visceral, physical joy he found in pure aesthetics.
Reflects his early naturalistic preference for endings that mirror the somber realities of life rather than escapism.
This word sets the high-stakes emotional tone for the 'evil time' in which he lived and wrote.
Orwell identifies this as the first primary motive, candidly acknowledging the self-centered pride inherent in authorship.
This sharp, dismissive term shows his impatience with dishonesty regarding a writer's true, often selfish, motivations.
A powerful image for the emotional death of the individual under the weight of daily drudgery and lack of ambition.
Represents the positive emotional drive found in the perception of beauty and the right arrangement of words.
A crucial word for Orwell, acknowledging the inescapable emotional and political slant present in all art.
One of the most intense words used, describing his deep-seated emotional reaction to authority and imperialism.
Used in his poem to describe the era he lived in, this word carries a heavy weight of moral despair and urgency.
Evokes a sense of loss and restriction, signaling a shift where personal 'dreams' must be sacrificed for political reality.
A violent, graphic verb used to describe how people are forced to damage their own joys to survive the era.
Identifies the emotional starting point of his writing: the feeling of taking a side in the face of injustice.
Orwell explicitly cites this emotion as the necessary fuel for his work, specifically regarding the truth of the Spanish Civil War.
Directly describes the grueling emotional and physical toll that the process of writing a book takes on him.
A metaphorical representation of the inexplicable, driving force that compels a writer to undergo the agony of creation.
Connects the high-minded act of writing back to the primal, selfish emotional needs of an infant.
Describes the emotional state of his work when it lacks a clear political purpose, representing failure to connect.
